annotations.label: Equality

anchor.snippet: equality is a relationship between two quantities or expressions

annotations.note: Propositional equality is Lean core's `Eq`, the foundational equality relation underlying all of Mathlib.

annotations.label: Equation or identity

anchor.snippet: A written expression of equality is called an equation or identity depending on the context

annotations.note: This is a terminological distinction about written expressions, not a formalizable mathematical statement.

annotations.label: Distinct objects

anchor.snippet: Two objects that are not equal are said to be distinct

annotations.note: Inequality/distinctness is Lean core's `Ne a b := ¬(a = b)`.

annotations.label: Reflexivity of equality

anchor.snippet: For every a , one has a = a

annotations.note: Reflexivity is the constructor `Eq.refl`/`rfl` in Lean core.

annotations.label: Symmetry of equality

anchor.snippet: if a = b , then b = a

annotations.note: Symmetry of equality is `Eq.symm` in Lean core.

annotations.label: Transitivity of equality

anchor.snippet: if a = b and b = c , then a = c

annotations.note: Transitivity of equality is `Eq.trans` in Lean core.

annotations.label: Substitution property

anchor.snippet: a can replace b in any mathematical expression or formula without changing its meaning

annotations.note: The substitution (Leibniz) property is `Eq.subst`/`▸` in Lean core.

annotations.label: Function application property

anchor.snippet: For every a and b , with some function

annotations.note: a = b implies f a = f b is `congrArg` in Lean core.

annotations.label: Equation

anchor.snippet: An equation is a symbolic equality of two mathematical expressions

annotations.note: An equation is a propositional equality `a = b`, represented by core `Eq`.

annotations.label: Algebra

anchor.snippet: Algebra is the branch of mathematics concerned with equation solving

annotations.note: This describes a branch of mathematics, not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Solution of an equation

anchor.snippet: is called a solution of the given equation

annotations.note: Mathlib has no general notion of 'solution'; only domain-specific ones such as `Polynomial.IsRoot` exist.

annotations.label: Solution set

anchor.snippet: is called its solution set

annotations.note: A solution set is expressible as `{x | equation x}` via `setOf`, but there is no dedicated 'solution set' concept.

annotations.label: Conditionally true equation

anchor.snippet: Equations with unknowns are considered conditionally true

annotations.note: This is an informal characterization of equations with free variables, not a formalized result.

annotations.label: Equation as binary predicate

anchor.snippet: an equation is a binary predicate

annotations.note: `Eq : α → α → Prop` is exactly a binary predicate.

annotations.label: Equation in computer science

anchor.snippet: an equation is defined as a boolean -valued expression

annotations.note: Boolean-valued equality is `BEq` (`==`) / `DecidableEq` in Lean core.

annotations.label: Identity

anchor.snippet: An identity is an equality that is true for all values of its variables in a given domain

annotations.note: Identities are universally-quantified equalities (∀ x, f x = g x) expressible in Lean, but 'identity' is not a named concept.

annotations.label: Extensionality of functions

anchor.snippet: identities may be viewed as an equality of functions

annotations.note: Function extensionality is `funext` in Lean core.

annotations.label: Functional equation

anchor.snippet: called a functional equation

annotations.note: Specific functional equations are formalized (e.g. the Riemann zeta functional equation) but 'functional equation' is not a defined notion.

annotations.label: Differential equation

anchor.snippet: A functional equation involving derivatives is called a differential equation

annotations.note: ODE infrastructure (HasDerivAt-based solutions, Grönwall, Picard–Lindelöf) exists but 'differential equation' is not defined as a term.

annotations.label: Equal by definition

anchor.snippet: which is called "equal by definition"

annotations.note: Definitional equality (`:=`) is a meta-level notion of the type theory, not a formalized declaration.

annotations.label: Definition of Euler's number

anchor.snippet: defines Euler's number

annotations.note: Euler's number is `Real.exp 1`, but there is no named constant defined via the limit (1+1/n)^n.

annotations.label: Extension by definition

anchor.snippet: this is called an extension by definition

annotations.note: Conservative extension by definition is a metalogical notion, not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Identity of indiscernibles

anchor.snippet: its converse , the identity of indiscernibles

annotations.note: The converse of Leibniz's law (shared properties ⇒ equal) is not formalized as a principle in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Law of identity

anchor.snippet: Stating that each thing is identical with itself, without restriction

annotations.note: The law of identity (a = a) is `Eq.refl`/`rfl` in Lean core.

annotations.label: Substitution property (axiom)

anchor.snippet: if two things are equal, then any property of one must be a property of the other

annotations.note: Indiscernibility of identicals is `Eq.subst`/`▸` in Lean core.

annotations.label: Function application deducible from axioms

anchor.snippet: Function application is also sometimes included in the axioms of equality

annotations.note: The function-application property is `congrArg`, derivable from substitution in Lean core.

annotations.label: Substitution failing in intensional context

anchor.snippet: The substitution property can produce false statements when applied naively

annotations.note: Failure of substitution in intensional natural-language contexts is not a formalizable mathematical statement.

annotations.label: Derivation of reflexivity

anchor.snippet: Reflexivity: Given any expression

annotations.note: Reflexivity is the primitive constructor `Eq.refl`/`rfl` in Lean core.

annotations.label: Derivation of symmetry

anchor.snippet: Symmetry: Given

annotations.note: Symmetry is proven as `Eq.symm` in Lean core.

annotations.label: Derivation of transitivity

anchor.snippet: Transitivity: Given

annotations.note: Transitivity is proven as `Eq.trans` in Lean core.

annotations.label: Derivation of function application

anchor.snippet: Function application: Given some function

annotations.note: Function application is proven as `congrArg` in Lean core.

annotations.label: Set theory

anchor.snippet: Set theory is the branch of mathematics that studies sets

annotations.note: This describes a branch of mathematics, not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Set equality by elements (extensionality)

anchor.snippet: two sets that have precisely the same elements are equal

annotations.note: `Set.ext` states sets with the same members are equal.

annotations.label: Set builder equality example

anchor.snippet: greater than 0 but not more than 3 is equal to the set containing only 1, 2, and 3

annotations.note: This is an ad hoc illustrative example, not a named declaration.

annotations.label: Intensional definition

anchor.snippet: an intensional definition describes the necessary and sufficient conditions for a term to apply to an object

annotations.note: This is a philosophical/logical notion of definition, not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Extensional definition

anchor.snippet: An extensional definition instead lists all objects where the term applies

annotations.note: This is a philosophical/logical notion of definition, not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Extension of a predicate

anchor.snippet: the extension of a predicate is the set of all objects for which the predicate is true

annotations.note: `setOf p` is exactly the set of all objects for which predicate `p` holds.

annotations.label: Axiom of extensionality (FOL with equality)

anchor.section: Set equality based on first-order logic with equality
anchor.snippet: two sets that contain the same elements are the same set

annotations.note: `Set.ext` is the extensionality principle for sets.

annotations.label: Set equality (FOL without equality)

anchor.section: Set equality based on first-order logic without equality
anchor.snippet: two sets are defined to be equal if they contain the same elements

annotations.note: Mathlib characterizes set equality by membership via `Set.ext`/`Set.ext_iff`.

annotations.label: Proof of reflexivity (sets)

anchor.snippet: Reflexivity: Given a set

annotations.note: Set equality is `Eq`, so reflexivity is `rfl`/`Eq.refl`.

annotations.label: Proof of symmetry (sets)

anchor.snippet: Symmetry: Given sets

annotations.note: Set equality is `Eq`, so symmetry is `Eq.symm`.

annotations.label: Proof of transitivity (sets)

anchor.snippet: Transitivity: Given sets

annotations.note: Set equality is `Eq`, so transitivity is `Eq.trans`.

annotations.label: Proof of function application (sets)

anchor.snippet: This is the defining property of an ordered pair

annotations.note: The defining property of ordered pairs, (a,b)=(c,d) ↔ a=c ∧ b=d, is `Prod.ext_iff`/`Prod.mk.injEq` in core.

annotations.label: Numerical analysis

anchor.snippet: Numerical analysis is the study of constructive methods and algorithms to find numerical approximations

annotations.note: This describes a branch of mathematics, not a formalizable statement.

annotations.label: Approximate equality not an equivalence relation

anchor.snippet: any rigorous definition of it will not be an equivalence relation, due to its not being transitive

annotations.note: No approximate-equality relation with a proof of its non-transitivity is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Floating-point number

anchor.snippet: Each floating-point number is represented as a significand

annotations.note: `FP.Float` (with `FloatCfg` and `ValidFinite`) models floating-point numbers by significand and exponent.

annotations.label: Undecidability of real number equality (Richardson's theorem)

anchor.snippet: the equality of two real numbers given by an expression is known to be undecidable

annotations.note: Richardson's theorem is listed in docs/1000.yaml without an associated declaration, i.e. not yet formalized.

annotations.label: Equivalence relation

anchor.snippet: An equivalence relation is a mathematical relation that generalizes the idea of similarity or sameness

annotations.note: Reflexive/symmetric/transitive relations are `Equivalence` (and bundled as `Setoid`) in Lean core.

annotations.label: Equivalence relations partition a set

anchor.snippet: These properties are enough to partition a set into disjoint equivalence classes

annotations.note: `Setoid.isPartition_classes` proves the equivalence classes of a setoid form a partition.

annotations.label: Equality is the strictest equivalence relation

anchor.snippet: it is the strictest possible equivalence relation

annotations.note: Equality is the bottom (finest) element of the complete lattice of equivalence relations, `Setoid.bot_def`.

annotations.label: Congruence relation

anchor.snippet: a congruence relation extends the idea of an equivalence relation to include the function-application property

annotations.note: `Con` extends `Setoid` with multiplication-compatibility (the function-application property); analogous `AddCon`/`RingCon` exist per structure.

annotations.label: Isomorphism

anchor.snippet: An isomorphism describes a kind of structure-preserving correspondence between two objects

annotations.note: `CategoryTheory.Iso` is the general structure-preserving (invertible-morphism) notion of isomorphism.

annotations.label: Isomorphism (formal)

anchor.snippet: an isomorphism is a bijective mapping

annotations.note: `Equiv` (α ≃ β) is a bijective mapping; structure-specific variants (e.g. `MulEquiv`) add structure preservation.

annotations.label: Group isomorphism

anchor.snippet: in group theory , a group isomorphism

annotations.note: `MulEquiv` (`≃*`) is a multiplication-preserving bijection, i.e. a group isomorphism.

annotations.label: Cyclic groups isomorphic to integers

anchor.snippet: all cyclic groups of order

annotations.note: `zmodMulEquivOfGenerator` gives a cyclic group of order n ≅ Multiplicative (ZMod n); `mulEquivOfCyclicCardEq` shows any two cyclic groups of equal cardinality are isomorphic.

annotations.label: Isomorphic vector spaces

anchor.snippet: two vector spaces are isomorphic if they have the same dimension

annotations.note: `LinearEquiv.nonempty_equiv_iff_rank_eq` states two (free) modules are linearly isomorphic iff they have equal rank/dimension.

annotations.label: Geometric equality of figures

anchor.snippet: two figures are equal if they contain exactly the same points

annotations.note: Mathlib has no dedicated 'figure' type; figures-as-point-sets are equal by `Set.ext` when they share all points.

annotations.label: Euclid's Pythagorean theorem statement

anchor.snippet: the square on the hypotenuse is equal to the squares on the sides, taken together

annotations.note: The Pythagorean theorem is formalized as an iff with the right-angle condition; Euclid's statement is its forward direction.

annotations.label: Archimedes' area of a circle

anchor.snippet: a circle is equal to the rectangle whose sides are the radius and half the circumference

annotations.note: The area of a disk (πr², equal to r·(C/2)) is the dimension-2 special case of `EuclideanSpace.volume_ball`.

annotations.label: Divisibly equal and equal in content

anchor.snippet: if they can be cut into finitely many triangles which are congruent

annotations.note: `Equidecomp` formalizes equidecomposition via a group action, generalizing scissors-congruence, but the Bolyai–Gerwien result is not present.

annotations.label: Similar figures

anchor.snippet: Similar figures are those that have the same shape but can differ in size

annotations.note: `Similar` defines point configurations as similar when their pairwise distances agree up to a nonzero scale factor.

annotations.label: Equipollence

anchor.snippet: a concept of equality of directed line segments , equipollence

annotations.note: No equipollence relation on directed segments is defined; Mathlib handles vectors directly via modules/affine torsors.
